analysis.overall_assessment: The image shows one leaf with a small hole, indicating some level of damage or disease.

primary_pollution_source: Acid rain or excess nitrogen fertilizers
pollution_reduction_solutions: Implement proper air filtration systems to reduce O3 exposure.
Use sulfur-oxygen compounds and nitrogen fertilizers in moderation to minimize SO₂ and NOx emissions.
Employ sustainable agricultural practices to prevent acid rain or excess nitrogen fertilizer use.
Avoid using harmful pesticides, herbicides, or chemicals. Opt for organic alternatives.
Ensure proper storage and handling of all chemicals used in the field.

immediate_actions: Inspect other leaves for similar damage
processing_recommendations.sorting_strategy: Sort leaves by size and appearance, separating healthy from damaged ones.
processing_recommendations.processing_method: Use gentle processing methods to avoid further stress on the leaves.
quality_preservation: Handle leaves with care during sorting
Avoid using harsh chemicals or heat in processing

waste_prevention.salvageable_portions: The undamaged parts of the leaf can still be used for certain applications.
alternative uses: Use damaged leaves as compost or mulch
Create natural dyes from tea leaves
waste_prevention.composting_guidelines: Compost affected leaves separately to avoid contaminating healthy ones.
prevention_measures: Regularly inspect plants for disease or damage, and take action promptly

economic_impact.estimated_loss_percentage: The loss is minimal as only one leaf is affected.
cost_saving_opportunities: Invest in better air filtration systems
value_recovery_methods: Use damaged leaves for compost or natural dyes

priority_actions: Inspect other leaves and plants regularly
recommendations.timeline: Begin immediate actions, follow-up in 1 month.
